MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Morrow-Meadows Corporation Motion For Summary Judgment
Off Calendar Dec-30-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Tiega Varlack, Esq. 510-397-2008 of Varlack Legal Services for Plaintiff.
Cynthia Shambaugh, Esq. 559-451-2630 x4 of the law Office of Thomas Burns for Defendant Ranger Pipelines through Court Call.
S. Christian Anderson, Esq. 650-989-5400 of Clapp Moroney Vucinich Beeman Scheley for Cross-Defendant Morrow-Meadows.

RULING - A R G U E D. Mr. Anderson argues. Mr. Varlack responds. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

CROSS DEFENDANT MORROW-MEADOWS CORPORATION's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Morrow-Meadows Corporation's motion for summary judgment is denied. Morrow-Meadows fails to meet its initial burden. (See Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 849 [a defendant moving for summary judgment "bears the burden of persuasion that one or more elements of the cause of action in question cannot be established, or that there is a complete defense thereto."].) Morrow-Meadows has not established there are no triable issues of fact as to whether it is liable to Plaintiff.

Morrow-Meadows owed a duty of care to Plaintiff. Section IV, subdivision (i) of the contract between Morrow-Meadows and Ranger Pipelines, Inc. specifically states, "Subcontractor shall provide safe and sufficient facilities to owner, engineer, contractor, or their duly authorized representatives for inspection of the work by subcontractor." (Plaintiff's Exh. E.) Thus, Morrow-Meadows had a contractual duty to maintain a safe facility, including a duty to remove any rubbish or surplus material that may have accumulated on the job site. (See, e.g. SeaBright Ins. Co. v. US Airways, Inc. (2011) 52 Cal.4th 590, 594 [hirer implicitly delegates to the contractor any tort law duty to ensure the safety of the specific workplace that is subject of the contract].)

Morrow-Meadows argues that there is no evidence to establish it committed any negligent act to breach a duty of care. However, the Court finds there is a triable issue of fact regarding whether Morrow-Meadows breached its duty of care when it failed to remove a ladder that was allegedly placed in an unsafe position, blocking a narrow walkway. (Li Decl., Ex. 5, p. 62-63; UMF 9-11.) There are numerous questions of fact to be determined at trial, such as whether the ladder was placed in a dangerous position and whether the failure to remove the ladder created an unsafe working environment for Plaintiff and others.

Privette and its progeny do not apply. The Privette doctrine generally holds that when an employee of an independent contractor hired to do dangerous work suffers a work-related injury, the employee cannot recover from the person who retained or hired the independent contractor. (Privette v. Superior Court (1993) 5 Cal.4th 689, 692; Johnson v. The Raytheon Co., Inc. (2019) 33 Cal.App.5th 617, 624; see also Ruiz v. Herman Weissker, Inc. (2005) 130 Cal.App.4th 52 [extending Privette rule to hirer's agent].) Here, Morrow-Meadows is not the general contractor or hirer, and Morrow-Meadows' affirmative contribution is not at issue. (See Hooker v. Department of Transportation (2002) 27 Cal.4th 198, 202 [a hirer may be liable to an employee of a contractor insofar as a hirer's exercise of retained control affirmatively contributed to the employee's injuries].)

Counsel for the Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling in compliance with CRC 3.1312. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript)
